answers.intake_q1: When did your back pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly your back pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury or trauma to your back or ribs?
answers.intake_q4: Any fever, chills, or unexplained weight loss since the pain started?
answers.intake_q5: Any difficulty breathing, coughing, or chest pain associated with your back pain?
answers.intake_q6: Have you noticed any numbness, weakness, or tingling in your legs or feet?
answers.intake_q7: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your back pain, and if so, what was the effect?
answers.intake_a7: I tired tylenol and it didn't help
answers.intake_q8: Any history of kidney problems or urinary symptoms like pain or blood in urine?
answers.intake_q9: Have you noticed any swelling, bruising, or tenderness when touching the area of your back pain?
answers.intake_q10: Any history of osteoporosis, cancer, or recent infections?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with left-sided lower rib cage back pain that started three weeks ago. She denies any recent injury or trauma, fever, chills, unexplained weight loss, difficulty breathing, coughing, chest pain, numbness, weakness, or tingling in her legs or feet. She reports tenderness on palpation of the affected area. She has tried acetaminophen without relief. There is no history of kidney problems, urinary symptoms, osteoporosis, cancer, or recent infections. Patient is not pregnant., and she has no known medication allergies. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no vital signs or physical exam findings available beyond patient-reported tenderness.

A: 
The working diagnosis is musculoskeletal back pain localized to the left lower rib cage, likely mechanical in nature given the absence of systemic symptoms or neurologic deficits. 

Differential: 
1. Musculoskeletal strain or sprain 
2. Costochondritis or rib inflammation 
3. Intercostal muscle strain 
4. Early presentation of a visceral cause such as renal or pleural pathology (less likely given lack of systemic symptoms)

P: 
 - First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. 
 - Alternatives: Acetaminophen may be used if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated, although the patient has already tried acetaminophen without relief. 
 - Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with renal or liver disease, gastrointestinal risk, or cardiovascular disease; this patient has no known renal or liver disease. Avoid NSAIDs if any contraindications develop. 
 - Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if she develops fever, chills, unexplained weight loss, worsening or spreading pain, new numbness or weakness in the legs or feet, difficulty breathing, chest pain, or urinary symptoms. 
 - Testing: No imaging or laboratory testing is indicated at this time given the absence of red flag symptoms. Reassess if symptoms worsen or fail to improve with conservative management.

evidence.title: AAFP Choosing Wisely: Imaging for Low Back Pain (Red Flags)
evidence.snippet: Information Low back pain is one of the most common reasons for an outpatient visit.
